title: The pale vegan with paresthesias she didn't tell anyone about
blurb: A macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils — and a posterior column that's slowly going dark. The one lab that pins it: methylmalonic acid.

patient.complaint: Fatigue, sore tongue, and 'pins and needles' in both feet for several months.

opening: 52-year-old strict vegan for 9 years. Fatigue and a beefy, sore tongue for ~3 months. Tonight she came in after nearly falling on the stairs — she 'can't tell where her feet are' in the dark. CBC: Hgb 8.4, MCV 116, smear shows hypersegmented PMNs and macro-ovalocytes. LDH 880. Indirect bilirubin elevated. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Strict vegan ~9 years (no eggs, no dairy, no fortified foods). No alcohol. No metformin, no nitrous oxide, no PPI / H2 blocker. No GI surgery. Menses regular. Family history non-contributory.
exam: HR 96, BP 118/72, RR 16, T 36.7, SpO2 98%. Pale conjunctivae, beefy glossitis, no scleral icterus today. Cardiac S1S2 no murmur. Neuro: vibration and proprioception lost to the knees bilaterally (posterior columns); positive Romberg; brisk reflexes; bilateral Babinski. Mini-mental status 27 / 30 with mild executive slowing.

prompts.cause: Which folate-cycle enzyme step is functionally blocked?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why do the neurologic findings happen here but NOT in pure folate deficiency, even though both cause megaloblastic anemia?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the folate-cycle enzyme step whose B12 cofactor is missing.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — methionine synthase (MS). Without methylcobalamin, MS can't remethylate homocysteine to methionine. 5-methyl-THF is trapped (the 'methyl trap'), homocysteine rises, and methionine / SAM falls — hence the megaloblastic anemia AND the demyelinating neuropathy.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The cofactor that's missing here is methylcobalamin (B12). Which enzyme uses it to hand a methyl group from 5-methyl-THF onto homocysteine?
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: MS (B12) — Homocysteine → Methionine. The 'methyl-folate trap' explains why this looks like a folate problem in the blood. The methylmalonic acid elevation and the neuropathy are why it isn't.

mcqs.stem: Why is methylmalonic acid elevated in B12 deficiency but NOT in pure folate deficiency?
choices: B12 (adenosylcobalamin) is the cofactor for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase; without it, methylmalonyl-CoA can't become succinyl-CoA
B12 deficiency directly degrades carnitine to MMA
MMA is filtered freely in folate deficiency
mcqs.explanation: Two B12-dependent enzymes: methionine synthase (methylcobalamin, cytosol) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (adenosylcobalamin, mitochondria). Lose B12 → both stall → homocysteine ↑ AND MMA ↑. Folate deficiency hits only homocysteine. MMA is the discriminator and the marker of TRUE B12 deficiency.

mcqs.stem: Why does B12 deficiency cause posterior-column and corticospinal myelopathy ('subacute combined degeneration'), but folate deficiency does not?
choices: Methylmalonyl-CoA / methylmalonic acid disrupts odd-chain fatty acid handling and myelin synthesis; SAM deficiency impairs myelin basic protein methylation
Folate deficiency damages anterior horn cells exclusively
B12 is required for axonal transport of GABA
Folate replaces B12 in myelin sheaths
mcqs.explanation: Two converging hits in myelinated tracts: (1) MMA accumulation interferes with normal long-chain fatty-acid synthesis used for myelin; (2) methionine / SAM falls → myelin basic protein methylation falters. Folate deficiency does not block either step — that's why pure folate deficiency does NOT cause the neuro syndrome, even though it does cause the megaloblastic anemia.

mcqs.stem: If you can't tell whether this is dietary B12 deficiency vs pernicious anemia, what would help differentiate?
choices: Anti-intrinsic-factor antibodies and a careful dietary / GI history (or methylmalonic acid response to oral B12)
mcqs.explanation: Anti-IF antibodies (highly specific, ~50% sensitive) and anti-parietal-cell antibodies (sensitive, less specific) plus history (vegan vs gastric surgery / atrophic gastritis / autoimmune disease) drive the workup. The Schilling test is largely historical now.
